tasks.title: LLM Router — Complexity Scoring Engine
tasks.details:
Design and implement the complexity scoring subsystem that feeds the LLM Router with a normalized 0.0–1.0 score used to decide backend selection. The engine must analyze every incoming user prompt and extract multiple orthogonal features: raw and estimated token counts, presence and density of code blocks or inline code, occurrence of reasoning-oriented keywords ("explain", "step-by-step", "prove"), mathematical symbols and expressions, explicit multi-step instructions, nested lists or numbered sequences, and other structural patterns that correlate with higher reasoning load.

Implement a modular scoring pipeline composed of independent heuristic modules that each produce a partial score. These modules are registered at startup and can be individually enabled or disabled via configuration. The pipeline aggregates weighted partial scores into a final normalized value. All heuristics must be deterministic and side-effect free so that identical prompts always yield identical scores across runs and machines.

Provide an extension point for future ML-based scoring models (e.g., a small distilled classifier or regression head) that can be swapped in without altering the router contract. The engine must integrate directly with the CPU prompt preprocessor so that scoring occurs once per prompt before any routing decision is made.

Expose configuration for per-heuristic weights, global scaling factors, and clamping thresholds so operators can tune behavior without recompilation. Emit structured logs and telemetry for every scoring operation, including per-heuristic contributions and final score, to support offline analysis and A/B testing of weight sets.

Target sub-millisecond latency (<1 ms P99) on typical prompt lengths by using only cheap string and regex operations, avoiding allocations in the hot path, and providing a fast-path for very short prompts.
tasks.testStrategy: Comprehensive unit tests for each heuristic module with manually curated prompt fixtures covering edge cases (empty, pure code, pure math, long multi-step, mixed). Property-based tests asserting score monotonicity (adding complexity never decreases score) and determinism (same input → same output). Golden-file regression tests storing known prompt→score pairs to detect unintended drift. Micro-benchmark suite ensuring P99 latency stays under 1 ms on representative prompt corpus.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Time‑of‑Day & Schedule Logic
tasks.details:
Design and implement the time‑aware routing subsystem that allows the LLM Router to select different backends or routing profiles based on the current wall‑clock time. The subsystem must support arbitrary configurable time windows (night mode 22:00–06:00, work hours 09:00–17:00, off‑peak 02:00–05:00, etc.) expressed in both 12‑hour and 24‑hour formats, with optional day‑of‑week masks. Multiple named routing profiles (night profile, heavy‑compute profile, privacy profile, cost‑saving profile) can be defined; each profile carries its own set of backend preferences, complexity thresholds, and fallback chains.

Implement a schedule evaluator that, given the current timestamp and the loaded schedule configuration, deterministically returns the single active routing profile (or “default” when no schedule matches). The evaluator must be side‑effect free and complete in <1 ms so it can be called on every prompt without measurable latency impact. The CPU pipeline will inject the current timestamp into RoutingContext before invoking the router, guaranteeing the schedule decision always reflects real time.

Support hot‑reload of the schedule configuration (TOML/JSON) without restarting the process, using file‑watch or explicit reload commands. Allow user overrides via a temporary “suspend schedule until <time>” or “force profile X for next N prompts” mechanism that takes precedence over the calendar while still logging the override for auditability.

Emit structured telemetry on every schedule evaluation: active profile, matched window, evaluation latency, and any override in effect. Plan ahead for future calendar features such as holiday blackouts, recurring “do not disturb” blocks, and per‑user personal schedules stored in the user profile store.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for the schedule evaluator covering every combination of overlapping windows, 12 h vs 24 h formats, day‑of‑week masks, and DST edge cases. Property tests verifying that the evaluator is deterministic and that exactly one profile is returned for any timestamp. Golden‑file tests storing known schedule configurations and their expected active profiles at specific timestamps. Micro‑benchmarks confirming P99 latency <1 ms on a 100‑window schedule.

tasks.title: LLM Router — System Load & Telemetry Integration
tasks.details:
Design and implement the system‑load awareness subsystem that supplies the LLM Router with real‑time metrics so routing decisions can react to hardware pressure. The collector must continuously sample GPU utilization percentage, VRAM used vs total, CPU load average, and system/chipset temperature without blocking the main inference path. In parallel it must measure round‑trip latency to every configured LAN Ollama node and remote inference endpoint, maintaining a rolling average and last‑known RTT for each target.

Implement a lightweight, non‑blocking telemetry collector (background thread or async task) that publishes snapshots into a lock‑free ring buffer or channel. The router simply reads the most recent snapshot when building RoutingContext; the collector never blocks prompt handling. All metrics are exposed through a stable, read‑only API (`get_latest_telemetry()`) that the pure routing function can call without side effects.

Support configuration‑driven thresholds (GPU > 85 %, VRAM > 90 %, RTT > 40 ms) that can be hot‑reloaded at runtime. When thresholds are crossed the router may deprioritize or entirely avoid a backend. Log every telemetry reading that influences a routing decision so post‑mortem analysis can reconstruct why a particular backend was chosen or rejected.

Design the collector to be deterministic from the router's point of view: given the same snapshot, the routing decision is reproducible. Plan for future metrics such as disk I/O saturation, network throughput, model queue depth, and per‑model memory footprint so the same telemetry framework can grow without API changes.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for the telemetry snapshot struct and ring‑buffer accessors. Integration tests that inject synthetic GPU, VRAM, CPU, temperature, and latency traces and verify the router reacts correctly to threshold crossings. Golden‑file tests capturing known load scenarios and the expected routing outcomes. Stress tests ensuring the collector never blocks the CPU pipeline even under 100 % GPU load.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Backend Health Checking & Availability Layer
tasks.details:
Design and implement the health‑checking subsystem that continuously monitors the availability and quality of every LLM backend the router may select. The subsystem must perform periodic async health probes against the local RTX 3090 Ollama instance, every configured LAN Ollama node, the Gemini API, the Grok API, and any future cloud or local inference endpoints. Each probe records reachability, round‑trip latency, last successful response, recent error rate, a degraded flag, and capability tags (tool calling, vision, long context, etc.).

Define a unified HealthStatus struct that aggregates these signals into a single snapshot per backend. The health layer runs on a configurable interval (default 5 s) using non‑blocking async tasks so that health checks never stall prompt processing. Snapshots are published into a lock‑free store that the router reads when constructing RoutingContext, allowing the pure routing function to treat health data as an immutable input.

Expose a stable, read‑only API (`get_backend_health(LLMBackend)`) usable from anywhere in the system. Support config‑driven thresholds (error_rate > 5 %, latency > 2 s, unreachable for > 30 s) that mark a backend degraded or offline; these thresholds can be hot‑reloaded without restarting the process. Log every health‑state transition (healthy → degraded → offline → recovered) with timestamps and reason codes so operators can audit availability incidents.

Guarantee that from the router’s perspective the health layer is deterministic: given identical HealthStatus values the routing decision is reproducible. Plan for future enhancements such as exponential back‑off on failing endpoints, adaptive probe intervals based on recent stability, and per‑backend custom probe strategies (HEAD vs lightweight prompt vs OpenAI `/models` call).
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for HealthStatus construction, transition logic, and config parsing. Integration tests that inject simulated failures (timeouts, 5xx, connection refused) and verify correct state transitions and router behavior. Golden‑file tests capturing known health scenarios and expected routing outcomes. Stress tests ensuring the health checker never blocks the CPU pipeline even when every backend is slow or unreachable.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Configuration System & Hot‑Reloading
tasks.details:
Design and implement the configuration subsystem that powers the LLM Router and all of its supporting modules. The system must accept both TOML and JSON config files and expose a single RouterConfig struct containing every tunable parameter: complexity-scoring weights, time-of-day routing windows, GPU/CPU load thresholds, latency cutoffs, backend capability flags, fallback ordering, health-check intervals, and named schedule profiles.

Implement a file-watcher (using notify or equivalent) that detects changes to the config file(s) and triggers an atomic hot-reload. The reload must be fully atomic so that the router and its subsystems never observe a partially-loaded configuration; either the entire new config is swapped in or the old one remains. A validation layer must reject invalid configs with precise, human-readable error messages before any reload occurs.

Integrate the config system with the CPU pipeline so that RoutingContext always receives the latest validated configuration snapshot. All router subsystems—complexity engine, schedule logic, telemetry collector, and health checker—must read from the same central config source, eliminating drift between modules. Emit structured logs on every config load, reload, and validation failure, including file path, timestamp, and diff summary.

Guarantee that the config layer is deterministic, thread-safe, and imposes negligible overhead on the hot path. Plan for future extensions such as remote config sources (HTTP/S3), environment-variable overrides, and per-user configuration namespaces.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for TOML/JSON parsing, validation rules, and struct construction. Hot-reload tests that write new config files at runtime and verify atomic swap behavior. Validation tests covering every error case with expected messages. Golden-file tests storing known config files and their parsed RouterConfig representations to prevent regressions.

tasks.title: LLM Router — User Override & Session Preference System
tasks.details:
Design and implement the user override subsystem that gives users explicit control over which LLM backend is selected for their prompts. The system must support three tiers of overrides: one-shot overrides (apply only to the next message), session-level overrides (persist for the duration of the conversation until explicitly cleared), and persistent preferences stored in the user profile or config file that survive restarts.

Integrate override information directly into RoutingContext so the pure routing function can treat it as an immutable input. Overrides must always take precedence over schedule logic, complexity scoring, and system load metrics — unless the requested backend is currently unhealthy or lacks required capability flags (e.g., tool calling). When an override targets an unavailable backend, the router should fall back to the next viable option while still logging the conflict.

Define a strict resolution order that is evaluated on every prompt: 1) one-shot override (highest priority, cleared after use), 2) session override, 3) persistent user preference, 4) normal router decision. Provide commands or metadata hooks (e.g., /use grok, /prefer gemini, /clear-override) that users or skills can invoke to set or clear overrides at any tier.

Validate every override against current backend health and capability flags before accepting it. Emit structured logs for every override activation, deactivation, and conflict so that audit trails are complete. Guarantee that the override layer is deterministic, thread-safe, and imposes negligible overhead on the hot path. Plan for future extensions such as per-tool overrides (different backends for code vs. chat) and per-task routing preferences.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for the override resolution engine covering every combination of one-shot, session, and persistent preferences, including conflict and unavailability cases. Integration tests that simulate user commands and verify the correct backend is chosen on subsequent prompts. Golden-file tests storing known override scenarios and their expected resolution outcomes to prevent regressions.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Fallback Engine & Degradation Logic
tasks.details:
Design and implement the fallback subsystem that guarantees the router always returns a usable backend even when the primary choice is unavailable. The engine must evaluate a deterministic fallback chain that considers backend health, measured latency, current GPU load, cost tier, capability tags, and any active user overrides. Fallback logic must respect the established override precedence (one-shot > session > persistent > router decision) while still ensuring the final selection is healthy and capable.

Integrate fallback evaluation directly into the RoutingContext pipeline so that every prompt receives a fully resolved BackendSelection result. Define a BackendSelection struct that captures the chosen backend, the ordered list of fallbacks that were attempted, the reason code for each fallback step, the health snapshots that influenced the decision, and precise timestamps for auditability.

Support graceful degradation modes (e.g., “reduced capability mode”) that activate when only local or lower-tier models remain available, allowing the system to continue operating with limited features rather than failing outright. All fallback decisions must be deterministic and reproducible given the same inputs, enabling reliable testing and post-mortem analysis.

Expose config-driven fallback ordering and per-backend fallback rules that can be hot-reloaded alongside the rest of the router configuration. Emit structured logs for every fallback attempt, failure, and recovery so operators can trace exactly why a particular backend was selected. Guarantee that the fallback engine is thread-safe, imposes negligible overhead, and remains compatible with live config changes. Plan for future enhancements such as adaptive fallback based on recent success rates, probabilistic fallback for load balancing, and cost-aware fallback that minimizes spend while meeting latency SLOs.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for the fallback chain evaluator covering every combination of health, latency, load, cost, capability, and override states. Integration tests that simulate backend failures (timeouts, 5xx, connection refused, degraded) and verify the router correctly walks the fallback chain. Golden-file tests storing known failure scenarios and their expected BackendSelection outputs to prevent regressions.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Public API & Integration Layer
tasks.details:
Design and implement the public-facing API that exposes the LLM Router to the rest of the system. The primary entry point must be a clean, stable `Router::select_backend()` method that accepts a fully-populated RoutingContext and returns a BackendSelection result. The API must be async, lightweight, and safe to call from any point in the CPU pipeline without blocking prompt processing.

Define the BackendSelection struct to carry the chosen backend, the ordered fallback chain that was evaluated, scoring metadata (complexity, load, latency, schedule), health snapshots at decision time, override resolution details, and precise timestamps for every stage. This rich payload enables downstream subsystems to understand exactly why a particular backend was selected.

Integrate the router API with the CPU prompt preprocessor (so every prompt is routed before reaching any LLM node), the tool/skill/hook system (so hooks can observe or override routing), the memory subsystem (so user preferences are loaded into context), and the telemetry/logging layer (so every routing decision is recorded). The API must remain deterministic and side-effect free; the only observable outputs are the returned BackendSelection and structured log events.

Design clear error types for routing failures, invalid or incomplete RoutingContext, missing telemetry, or health data, allowing callers to handle edge cases gracefully. Ensure the API surface is future-proof: new backends, new routing strategies, and additional metadata fields can be added without breaking existing callers. Emit structured logs for every API invocation, including input context hash, selected backend, latency, and any warnings. Plan for future enhancements such as streaming routing decisions or parallel evaluation of multiple backends for A/B testing.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for the public API surface, error handling, and BackendSelection construction. Integration tests that exercise the router from the CPU pipeline, tool/skill/hook layer, and memory subsystem. Golden-file tests capturing known RoutingContext inputs and their expected BackendSelection outputs to guarantee long-term API stability.

tasks.title: LLM Router — End‑to‑End Integration Test Harness
tasks.details:
Design and implement a comprehensive end‑to‑end integration test harness that validates the entire LLM Router subsystem under realistic conditions. The harness must be capable of constructing fully‑populated RoutingContext objects with controlled, deterministic inputs for prompt complexity, timestamp, GPU load, LAN latency, backend health, user overrides, and schedule state. It then feeds these contexts to the router and asserts that the returned BackendSelection matches the expected outcome for each scenario.

Support a rich library of test scenarios covering low‑complexity prompts (should prefer cheap/fast backends), high‑complexity prompts (should route to high‑capability models), night‑mode windows, GPU overload conditions, LAN latency spikes, complete backend outages, active user overrides at every tier, degraded health states, and multi‑step fallback chains. Every scenario must be reproducible with fixed random seeds so CI runs are stable and deterministic.

Design golden‑file outputs that capture the complete BackendSelection (chosen backend, fallback chain, scoring metadata, health snapshots, override resolution, timestamps) for each scenario. These golden files serve as regression tests; any change in routing behavior must be explicitly reviewed. Integrate the harness with the real CPU pipeline so that end‑to‑end prompt flows can be exercised without requiring actual GPU hardware or network access.

Provide snapshot testing for all routing metadata so that changes in fallback order, scoring weights, or health evaluation are immediately visible. Emit structured logs during test execution that include the full RoutingContext and BackendSelection for every decision, enabling rapid debugging of failures. The harness must run in CI environments without GPU access by using fully mocked telemetry and health providers. Plan for future extensions such as multi‑backend parallel evaluation and probabilistic routing simulations.
tasks.testStrategy: Comprehensive integration tests exercising every major routing scenario. Golden‑file tests comparing BackendSelection outputs against stored expectations. Snapshot tests for routing metadata. CPU pipeline integration tests that run complete prompt flows through the router. All tests must be deterministic and runnable in CI without hardware dependencies.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Debugging, Inspection & Visualization Tools
tasks.details:
Design and implement a comprehensive developer-facing debugging and inspection toolkit for the LLM Router. The toolkit must provide a router_debug command (CLI) and an equivalent API endpoint that, given a prompt or RoutingContext, prints or returns the complete routing decision tree. The output must include the per-heuristic complexity score breakdown, the active time-of-day profile and schedule window, the full telemetry snapshot (GPU load, VRAM, CPU, per-endpoint latency, health status), the override resolution result at every tier, the ordered fallback chain with reason codes, and the final BackendSelection with timestamps.

Implement an explain mode that produces a human-readable, step-by-step narrative of the routing decision, similar to a compiler verbose output or a proof trace. This narrative must be deterministic and reproducible so that identical inputs always yield identical explanations. In parallel, emit a machine-readable JSON representation of the entire decision tree that is suitable for visualization tools, dashboards, or automated analysis.

Integrate the debugging layer with the existing structured logging subsystem so that any routing decision can be replayed offline by feeding the captured log record back into the inspector. Provide a lightweight TUI (text-based UI) or interactive CLI inspector that shows live router state, current health snapshots, and recent decisions without requiring a separate process. All debug output must be safe for production use: sensitive prompt text and user identifiers must be redacted or hashed by default.

Support the ability to save and reload debug snapshots (RoutingContext + BackendSelection + config) for regression testing and sharing. The inspection layer must be strictly read-only with respect to routing behavior; enabling debug mode must never alter the BackendSelection that the router would have returned. Plan for future extensions such as graphical web dashboards, WebSocket live feeds, and integration with observability platforms (OpenTelemetry, Prometheus).
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests verifying that explain-mode output and JSON visualization are deterministic and contain all required fields. Integration tests that exercise the debug command/API against the live router and confirm that routing behavior is unchanged. Golden-file tests storing known decision trees and their expected explain/JSON output to prevent regressions in the inspection layer.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Developer Documentation & Architecture Guide
tasks.details:
Create comprehensive, developer-facing documentation for the entire LLM Router subsystem. The documentation must begin with a high-level architecture overview that explains the router’s purpose, how it integrates as a pre-LLM decision layer in the CPU pipeline, how RoutingContext is assembled from prompt preprocessing, telemetry, schedule state, and user overrides, how the pure routing function produces a BackendSelection, and how the various subsystems (complexity scoring, schedule logic, load telemetry, health checking, fallback, and overrides) interact to reach a final decision.

Document each subsystem in detail: Complexity Scoring Engine (feature extraction, weighting, normalization), Time-of-Day & Schedule Logic (windows, profiles, hot-reload), System Load & Telemetry Integration (GPU/VRAM/CPU/latency collectors, lock-free snapshots), Backend Health Checking (probe strategies, HealthStatus, degradation thresholds), Configuration System & Hot-Reloading (RouterConfig, atomic reload, validation), User Override System (one-shot/session/persistent tiers, resolution order), Fallback Engine (deterministic chains, graceful degradation), Public Router API (select_backend contract, error types), and Debugging & Inspection Tools (explain mode, visualization JSON, TUI).

Include clear diagrams (ASCII art and Mermaid) showing data flow from prompt arrival through RoutingContext construction, routing decision, and BackendSelection return. Provide a “How to Add a New Backend” guide covering enum extension, capability flags, health probes, and config integration. Provide a “How to Tune Router Behavior” guide covering weights, thresholds, and schedule profiles. Provide a “How to Debug Routing Decisions” guide covering router_debug usage, snapshot replay, and log analysis. All documentation must contain concrete examples of RoutingContext inputs and corresponding BackendSelection outputs, remain deterministic and version-controlled, and be structured for easy navigation by new contributors. Plan for future automation such as doc generation from Rust doc comments and diagram regeneration from code.
tasks.testStrategy: Documentation linting (markdown, Mermaid syntax), link checking, example validation (ensure every code sample compiles or runs), and cross-subsystem consistency checks to guarantee that documented behavior matches implementation.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Performance Optimization & Latency Reduction
tasks.details:
Profile the entire LLM Router subsystem to identify and eliminate latency bottlenecks. Measure execution time of every major stage: complexity scoring (per-heuristic and aggregate), schedule evaluation, telemetry snapshot retrieval, health-check lookups, config access, fallback chain evaluation, and final backend selection. Establish a hard performance budget (e.g., router must complete in <2 ms P99 under normal load) and treat any regression beyond this budget as a failure.

Implement micro-optimizations where profiling indicates value: branch-predictor-friendly code layout, pre-computed threshold comparisons, lock-free reads, and minimal allocations on the hot path. Ensure all hot paths are allocation-free or use pre-allocated buffers that are reused across invocations. Add lightweight, deterministic caching layers for recent complexity scores, telemetry snapshots, health states, and schedule evaluations; caches must be invalidated correctly on config reload or health-state change and must never introduce non-determinism.

Create a performance test suite that runs thousands of synthetic routing scenarios at scale, recording P50/P95/P99 latencies and allocation counts. Integrate these metrics into the structured logging/telemetry system so that production deployments can be monitored for regression. Guarantee that every optimization preserves the deterministic contract: identical RoutingContext inputs must always produce identical BackendSelection outputs. Plan for future work such as adaptive routing or parallel evaluation that must not increase the critical-path latency.
tasks.testStrategy: Performance benchmarks and micro-benchmarks for every routing stage. Latency regression tests that fail CI on P99 increase. Stress tests under simulated high-load conditions with allocation and latency assertions. All tests must remain deterministic.

subtasks.title: Profile every routing stage and establish latency budget
subtasks.details: Measure complexity, schedule, telemetry, health, config, fallback, selection.

subtasks.details: Scale to thousands of scenarios, enforce budget.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Scenario Simulator & Routing Sandbox
tasks.details:
Design and implement a standalone scenario simulator that enables developers to exercise the LLM Router in isolation without launching the full bot or requiring GPU hardware. The simulator accepts scenario definitions expressed in JSON or TOML that fully describe a routing situation: prompt text, optional complexity-score overrides, wall-clock time, GPU/CPU/VRAM load, per-endpoint LAN latency, backend health states, active user overrides at any tier, and arbitrary config variations (weights, thresholds, schedules).

Implement a sandbox runner that loads a scenario file, materializes a deterministic RoutingContext, invokes the complete router pipeline (complexity scoring, schedule evaluation, telemetry/health lookup, override resolution, fallback logic), and produces a comprehensive routing explanation. The explanation must include the per-heuristic complexity breakdown, active schedule profile, telemetry snapshot, health evaluation, override resolution trace, ordered fallback chain with reasons, and the final BackendSelection with timestamps.

Support batch execution of scenario suites for regression testing and CI. Every simulation must be fully deterministic and reproducible given the same scenario file and router binary. Integrate the simulator with the debugging/inspection layer so that explain-mode output and visualization JSON are emitted automatically. The simulator must run in CI environments without GPU access by using fully mocked telemetry and health providers. Log every scenario result (input context, decision tree, BackendSelection) to structured files for later analysis or golden-file comparison. Plan for future extensions such as probabilistic routing simulation and multi-backend parallel evaluation under the same deterministic harness.
tasks.testStrategy: Scenario-based tests exercising the full router pipeline. Golden-file tests comparing simulator output against stored expectations. Regression suites that run on every commit. CI-safe simulations that require no hardware and produce deterministic results.

subtasks.title: Define scenario file format (JSON/TOML) for all routing inputs

tasks.title: LLM Router — Packaging, Versioning & Release Pipeline
tasks.details:
Package the entire LLM Router subsystem as a clean, modular internal crate or module that can be versioned and evolved independently while remaining tightly integrated with the CPU pipeline. Define a clear versioning strategy (semantic versioning or router-specific version tags) so that breaking changes, feature additions, and bug fixes are communicated consistently to consumers. Ensure the router can evolve without breaking the CPU pipeline, existing tools, or downstream skills by maintaining a stable public API surface and marking internal modules appropriately with Rust visibility rules.

Introduce feature flags that allow optional subsystems (telemetry collector, scenario simulator, debug/inspection tools, visualization JSON emitter) to be compiled in or out, enabling minimal or full-featured builds as needed. Integrate the router crate into the bot’s Cargo workspace and build pipeline so that any change to router code triggers the full test suite. Add automated CI jobs that run on every commit touching router code, exercising unit tests, integration tests, golden-file regressions, performance benchmarks, and health/fallback verification.

Create a formal release checklist that must be satisfied before any router release: config schema validation, golden-file routing regression tests, performance benchmark assertions, health-check stability tests, fallback chain verification, and documentation updates. Document the release process, versioning rules, and API stability guarantees so that future maintainers can execute releases confidently. Plan for future extensibility such as router plugins or dynamic strategy loading while preserving the deterministic, side-effect-free contract of the core routing function.
tasks.testStrategy: CI integration tests that run on every router change. Versioning regression tests that verify public API stability across releases. Release pipeline validation that enforces the checklist. API stability checks using cargo-semver-checks or equivalent tooling.

subtasks.title: Package router as modular crate with clear public/private boundaries
subtasks.details: Stable API surface, internal modules hidden.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Metrics, Analytics & Long‑Term Observability
tasks.details:
Design and implement a metrics and analytics subsystem that provides long-term visibility into the LLM Router’s behavior and health. The subsystem must continuously track and aggregate key operational signals: backend selection frequency (per-backend counts and percentages), fallback rates (how often the primary choice is unavailable), override usage patterns (one-shot vs session vs persistent), complexity score distributions, end-to-end routing latency histograms and percentiles, GPU load and LAN latency patterns, backend health state transitions, and routing errors or anomalies.

Define a stable MetricsRecord struct that captures aggregated data for a given time window. Implement rolling windows at multiple granularities (1 minute, 5 minutes, 1 hour, 24 hours, 7 days) so operators can observe both short-term spikes and long-term trends. Add histogram and percentile tracking (P50, P95, P99) for latency and complexity scores, enabling SLO monitoring and performance regression detection.

Integrate the metrics layer with the existing structured logging and telemetry systems so that every routing decision contributes to the aggregate view without blocking the hot path. Collection must be low-overhead and fully asynchronous. Design a pluggable exporter architecture supporting local JSON files, SQLite, Prometheus, and future backends, allowing the same metrics data to feed multiple observability platforms.

Provide a CLI/TUI command that displays real-time router metrics, current window aggregates, and recent trends. Ensure that from the router’s perspective all metrics collection is deterministic: identical inputs produce identical routing decisions regardless of whether metrics are enabled. Plan for future enhancements such as anomaly detection, automatic alerting on unusual fallback rates, and adaptive routing strategies informed by historical data.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for metrics aggregation, rolling windows, histograms, and percentile calculations. Integration tests for each exporter backend. Golden-file tests that capture known routing workloads and their expected MetricsRecord snapshots to prevent regression in analytics output.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Chaos Testing & Failure‑Mode Hardening
tasks.details: Design and implement a chaos-testing framework that subjects the LLM Router to extreme, unpredictable, and adversarial conditions to validate its resilience and correctness.
tasks.testStrategy: Fuzz tests and property-based tests that assert determinism and safety under chaos. Randomized scenario generators exercising multi-backend failures, health flapping, and override conflicts. CI reproducibility tests that run with fixed seeds. Golden-file validation of failure-mode behavior to ensure graceful degradation never regresses.

subtasks.details: Never panic, never invalid backend, always valid selection.

subtasks.details: Predictable failure modes with full context.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Final QA, Stabilization & Production Readiness
tasks.details:
Perform a comprehensive full-system QA pass across every router subsystem: complexity scoring engine, time-of-day & schedule logic, system load & telemetry integration, backend health checking, configuration system & hot-reloading, user override system, fallback engine, public Router API, debugging & inspection tools, scenario simulator, metrics & long-term observability, and chaos-testing framework. Validate that all subsystems behave consistently and correctly under normal load, high load, degraded conditions, partial failures, and complete backend outages.

Ensure every log message is structured, consistent, and contains sufficient context for production debugging. Verify that all configuration files, JSON/TOML schemas, example scenarios, and documentation are accurate and up to date. Execute the complete golden-file regression suite and regenerate baselines only when intentional behavior changes are confirmed.

Confirm that the router meets its documented performance budgets (<2 ms P99 under normal load) and latency targets across all hot paths. Conduct a final audit covering error handling completeness, panic safety, deterministic behavior under all inputs, thread safety, and memory-usage characteristics. Finalize all architecture diagrams, developer guides,

documents, and release checklists. Ensure the router is fully wired into the CPU pipeline with no remaining TODOs, warnings, or incomplete integrations. Prepare the subsystem for real-world usage by external tools, agents, or future modules, and define the post-launch monitoring and incremental-improvement roadmap.
tasks.testStrategy: Full-system QA exercising every subsystem under normal, high, degraded, and failure conditions. Complete golden-file regression validation. Performance benchmark verification against budgets. Production-readiness checklist covering logs, config, determinism, safety, documentation, and integration.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Configuration Schema & Default router.toml
tasks.description: Create the complete configuration schema for the LLM Router, including all router-specific settings, thresholds, backend definitions, schedules, fallback ordering, override rules, telemetry thresholds, metrics settings, simulator options, and chaos-testing toggles. This task establishes the authoritative configuration model that all router subsystems depend on. It includes designing a dedicated router.toml file (or router section in config.toml), implementing a strongly typed RouterConfig struct in Rust, adding schema validation, and ensuring all router subsystems read from this unified configuration source. The task also includes generating a full default router.toml with documented fields, integrating hot-reloading support, and ensuring atomic, thread-safe propagation of config updates.
tasks.details: This task defines the canonical configuration layer for the entire LLM Router. It requires designing a structured, hierarchical configuration schema that captures all router behavior in a deterministic and extensible way. The schema must include sections for complexity scoring weights, schedule windows, system load thresholds, backend capability flags, health-check intervals, fallback ordering, override rules, metrics and observability settings, simulator options, and chaos-testing toggles. The RouterConfig struct must be implemented with strong typing, default values, and full validation logic to prevent invalid or partial configurations from being loaded. The configuration loader must support hot-reloading via file watchers, ensuring atomic updates and thread-safe propagation of new settings. All router subsystems—including complexity scoring, schedule logic, telemetry integration, health checking, fallback engine, override system, and metrics—must be updated to read from this unified configuration source. The task also includes generating a complete example router.toml file with all fields documented and providing developer documentation for how to modify, extend, or tune router behavior through configuration.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for config parsing, validation, and defaulting; integration tests verifying that all router subsystems correctly read from RouterConfig; hot-reload tests ensuring atomic updates; golden-file tests for example router.toml; schema consistency tests; and failure-mode tests for invalid or partial configs.

Integrate RouterConfig into all router subsystems (complexity, schedule, telemetry, health, fallback, overrides, metrics).
Implement hot-reloading with atomic updates and thread-safe propagation.
Add structured error messages for invalid or missing configuration fields.
Write unit tests for parsing, validation, and defaulting behavior.
Write unit tests for parsing, validation, and defaulting behavior.
Write integration tests verifying correct config usage across router subsystems.
Add golden-file tests for router.toml and schema stability.
Document the entire configuration system and how developers can tune router behavior.

tasks.title: LLM Router — Configuration Validation & Enforcement Layer
tasks.description: Implement a strict validation and enforcement layer for the LLM Router configuration system. This includes validating all fields in router.toml (or the router section of config.toml), enforcing required constraints, rejecting invalid or inconsistent configurations, and ensuring the router never operates with partial or corrupted settings. The task includes building a validation pipeline that runs at startup and during hot-reload events, producing structured errors and preventing unsafe configurations from being applied. It also includes adding enforcement logic that guarantees deterministic behavior even when optional fields are missing, deprecated fields are present, or new schema versions are introduced.
tasks.details: This task introduces the runtime safety layer for the router configuration system. It requires implementing a multi-stage validation pipeline that checks all router configuration fields for correctness, type safety, logical consistency, and cross-field dependencies. The validation system must run both at startup and during hot-reload events, ensuring that invalid configurations never propagate into the router's internal state. The enforcement layer must guarantee deterministic behavior by applying defaults, rejecting invalid values, and producing structured, developer-friendly error messages.
tasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for field-level validation, cross-field validation, and schema consistency; integration tests verifying that invalid configs are rejected and valid configs are accepted; hot-reload tests ensuring atomic validation and safe fallback behavior; golden-file tests for error messages; and regression tests for schema evolution.

This includes introducing a versioned router configuration format, defining migration steps between versions, and providing automated tools to upgrade existing router.toml files to newer schemas without breaking behavior.
tasks.details: This task establishes a robust, version-aware migration system for the router configuration layer. It ensures that router.toml files can evolve safely as new fields, renamed fields, or removed fields are introduced.
